title: Home & Family
shortSummary: Talk about your home, your relatives, and family life, the heart of Filipino culture.
culturalNote: Family (pamilya) is the centre of Filipino life, and the family circle is wide: grandparents (lolo and lola), aunts and uncles (tito and tita), cousins (pinsan), and even close family friends are all woven in. Many households are multigenerational, with grandparents, parents, and children living under one roof, and respect for elders is paramount. Older siblings are addressed as kuya (older brother) and ate (older sister), titles also used warmly for strangers slightly older than you. Children are expected to show utang na loob (a deep sense of gratitude) to their parents, and caring for ageing parents at home is the norm rather than the exception. Sunday family gatherings, shared meals, and helping relatives are everyday expressions of this closeness.

keyPhrases.native: Kasama namin ang lolo at lola.
keyPhrases.english: Grandpa and grandma live with us.
keyPhrases.pronunciation: ka-SA-ma na-MIN ang LO-lo at LO-la

keyPhrases.english: I am the child of a farmer.

grammar.rule: Possession with the ng-pronouns ko, mo, niya
grammar.explanation: To show possession, Filipino places a pronoun after the noun: bahay ko (my house), pamilya mo (your family), anak niya (his/her child). These ng-pronouns mark 'my, your, his/her' and follow the thing owned.
grammar.commonMistake: Putting the pronoun before the noun, English-style, such as ko pamilya instead of pamilya ko.

grammar.rule: Existence and possession with may and mayroon
grammar.explanation: May means 'there is' or 'have' and comes before the noun: may kapatid ako (I have a sibling). Mayroon is the longer form used when the pronoun comes first: mayroon akong kapatid. Both express having something.
grammar.commonMistake: Using may directly before a pronoun; with a leading pronoun use mayroon, as in mayroon akong.

dialogue.setup: Joy shows her new friend Tonio a family photo.

lines.speaker: Joy
lines.native: Tingnan mo, ito ang pamilya ko.

lines.speaker: Tonio
lines.english: How lovely! How many siblings are you?

lines.speaker: Joy
lines.native: Tatlo kami. Si Kuya Jun ang panganay, ako ang bunso.
lines.english: We are three. Kuya Jun is the eldest, I am the youngest.

lines.speaker: Tonio
lines.native: Kasama ninyo ang lolo at lola?
lines.english: Do your grandpa and grandma live with you?

lines.speaker: Joy
lines.native: Oo, nakatira kaming lahat sa isang bahay.
lines.english: Yes, we all live in one house.

lines.speaker: Tonio
lines.native: Ang saya niyan! Masuwerte kang malapit kayo.
lines.english: That's wonderful! You're lucky to be close.

lines.speaker: Joy
lines.native: Totoo. Mahal na mahal ko ang pamilya ko.
lines.english: True. I love my family very much.

quiz.question: How do you say 'This is my family' in Filipino?
What does 'kuya' mean?
quiz.explanation: Kuya is older brother; ate is older sister.

quiz.question: Malaki ang bahay _____. (the word for 'my')
quiz.explanation: Ko means 'my' and follows the noun: bahay ko.

quiz.question: Which phrase correctly means 'I have two siblings'?
quiz.explanation: May expresses having; the linker -ng joins akong kapatid.

quiz.question: Arrange: ko / pamilya / mahal / ang
quiz.explanation: Mahal ko ang pamilya means 'I love my family'.
